TextENGNew: The community health examinations were of tremendous value to the progress of our research.
Limited though they were to residents above a certain age, they allowed us to examine a large number of Hinamizawa villagers at the same time.
...It was the greatest advantage of running a clinic as the institute's public face.
changeReason: Old loosely says 'perfect cover for our research center'; new accurately translates the advantage of running a clinic as the institute's public face (研究所が表向き、診療所をやっている).

TextENGNew: Furthermore, through a range of prior arrangements, we had also secured an exclusive right to perform autopsies on the remains of any villager who died.
...For someone who regarded medicine as a discipline of scholarship, there could not have been a more richly favoured environment.
changeReason: Old generalizes to 'those in the medical field'; new accurately captures 医学を学問と捉える者 (one who regards medicine as an academic discipline), specifying the researcher's perspective.

TextENGNew: The follow-up investigation of the late Dr. Takano's research materials was proceeding smoothly.

...The foresight it displayed was almost impossible to believe had come from a single individual's research—it was gradually revealing its full scope, and each time it did, I was reminded anew of just how remarkable that man's work had been.
changeReason: Old gives a generic summary ('astounding data, incredible researcher'); new accurately captures the Japanese about the research's foresight (先見性), its gradual revelation of scope, and the ongoing recognition of the late researcher's work.

TextENGNew: Excuse me, Director.

...Oh, Takano-san.
Thank you for your work.

The remaining test results have just arrived by post from Tokyo.
I will leave them here.

Thank you.
...Things are getting quite interesting, aren't they.

Hee hee.
I am very glad to be sharing this excitement with you.
...Takano-san was my assistant—a person seconded from the Ground Self-Defence Force.
changeReason: Old says generic 'Self-Defense Force'; new correctly specifies 'Ground Self-Defence Force' for 陸自 (陸上自衛隊).

TextENGNew: But her whole bearing was a far cry from the image of a Self-Defence Force officer that I held in my mind.

...Just as our research was concealed from every angle, both in appearance and in administrative terms—
...perhaps her true identity was being concealed as well.
changeReason: Old omits the specifics 外見的にも事務的にもあらゆる面から (in appearance, administratively, from every angle); new accurately captures this multi-dimensional concealment.

TextENGNew: ...The name Takano was in all probability an alias.
I was nothing more than an ordinary doctor—a researcher.
Without Takano-san's support, managing the Irie Institute as its director would have been entirely beyond me.

She was the conduit to our clients in Tokyo, my assistant—
...and also a researcher with extraordinarily deep knowledge of Hinamizawa Syndrome.
...Her presence was indispensable.
...The more I thought about it, the more it seemed as though it should properly have been called the Takano Institute rather than the Irie Institute—with her in the director's seat.
But this had been explained to me when I was first approached.
...Our clients wished to place a civilian at the head of the institute.
To that end they sought out someone who met their requirements—and found me, and installed me as director.
...I had some awareness that I was little more than a decorative figure on a ceremonial platform.
changeReason: Old uses 'puppet on their strings' which implies being controlled; new correctly captures 雛壇に飾られている (a decorative figure on a ceremonial platform), implying a figurehead role rather than manipulation.

TextENGNew: But I was not content to merely be an ornament.
I had been entrusted with the study of an exceedingly rare endemic disease.
changeReason: Old omits 風土病 (endemic disease), calling it just a 'rare disease'; new correctly identifies it as 'exceedingly rare endemic disease'.

TextENGNew: I too was a researcher.
Intellectual curiosity gnawed at me.
changeReason: Old loosely says 'I'm excited'; new accurately translates 知的好奇心が疼く as 'intellectual curiosity gnawed at me', which conveys the compulsive, almost uncomfortable nature of the curiosity.

TextENGNew: Whatever our clients' calculations, I harboured a genuine desire to unravel the mystery of Hinamizawa Syndrome—to be one of the first people to reach a truth unknown to humanity.

``...We can gather limitless indirect evidence suggesting that Hinamizawa Syndrome exists as a parasitic infection—
...yet we still cannot locate the pathogen responsible. It is deeply frustrating.''
changeReason: Old uses 'data' while new correctly renders 浮かし彫りの情報 as 'indirect evidence', and old says 'a parasite as the cause' while new more accurately says 'parasitic infection' for 寄生虫感染症.

TextENGNew:
That is true.
I had rather hoped an electron microscope would simply reveal it to us.
The virus—an existence so minute it cannot be seen through an ordinary optical microscope.
The invention of the electron microscope, which made such things observable, had been of immense significance to the world of medicine.
But it was prohibitively expensive, and emphatically not something a private individual could own.
The late Dr. Takano had predicted in his notes that an electron microscope would certainly be able to detect it—but that prediction had turned out to be wrong.

Takano-san too had apparently been optimistic that the electron microscope would easily expose the identity of the pathogen—
...and had been despondent at being unable to find anything across any of the autopsies she conducted.
In truth, it seemed our research had reached its first real impasse here—and for some weeks now, progress had ground to a halt.
...Because of that, we needed to think carefully about how to proceed.

...Then perhaps it is something even smaller than sub-microscopic.
Or it may be that there is a problem with the specimens themselves.
What do you mean by that?

For example—you are no doubt aware that wild mice often carry large numbers of fleas as parasites.
But if you examine the body of a dead mouse, you will not find any fleas.
That is because when the host dies, the living foundation of the parasite collapses along with it.
So—if the host dies, it is the same as the entire world ending for the parasite.

Exactly.
Just as you would not be able to find any humans in a world that had already perished.

...Which means we simply need to examine the world before it ends.
Hee hee.

That is so...
However, the world before it ends has human rights.
It would be difficult to examine it while it is still alive...
Examining the brain of a living human being.
...Even speaking of it in euphemism, the words carried an enormous weight.
From a purely medical standpoint, that would tell us what we needed to know.
...But we, living in human society, were bound by the rules and ethics of that society.
And it was within those constraints that we had to advance medicine...
I was reminded of a time when I had driven myself down the path of psychosurgery, convinced it was for the good of humanity.
changeReason: Old says 'studying lobotomies' while new correctly uses 'psychosurgery' (精神外科) and captures the driven, compulsive quality (突き進んでいた) that the old version lacks.

TextENGNew: ...I did not believe I had been wrong.
...But would the concept of ethics ever forgive me?
I did not know.
One day the judge of hell would render his verdict upon me.
changeReason: Old translates 地獄の閻魔 as 'the devil' which is a different entity; new correctly renders it as 'the judge of hell' (Enma, the Buddhist judge of the dead).

TextENGNew: Whether innocent or guilty—
...I intended to accept it without complaint.

I was gazing into the distance, turning over those old memories in my mind—
when that voice reached me, sudden and clear.
It was Takano-san's voice.
...Very clear, very straightforward—and for precisely that reason, it made me flinch.

Understood.
Allow me to prepare a world on the eve of its end.
...What?
I started.
...The phrase the advancement of medicine all too often leads down a path that whispers of willingness to accept sacrifices.
I had just heard that whisper of the devil.

``Hee hee.
Oh, it is not so very difficult.
I simply need to find a Hinamizawa resident currently hospitalised in serious condition—someone who could die at any moment.''
changeReason: Old omits that the patient must be 'hospitalized in serious condition' (重篤の状態で市内病院に入院); new includes this critical detail about the plan's specifics.

TextENGNew: ...But... even setting aside how brief their remaining time might be, would there really be anyone with the courage to offer their living body as a research specimen...?

There is no need to worry about that.
For someone who will die regardless, we are merely having them go to sleep a little sooner.
On paper, of course.
Hee hee.
Issue a death certificate for a patient who is still alive—and conduct the autopsy
while they yet live.

Th-that is impossible...!
In the first place, a major surgery such as a cranial incision would leave unmistakable traces.
If the family were to view the remains, they would certainly find it suspicious—

That is precisely the sort of thing we are here to manage.
I will have the Mountain Dogs immediately search the local hospital for a patient who meets our conditions.

``You need only sit in your chair in the laboratory and await good news.@k@vS20/09/VTK1_takano1286. I will bring your research specimen to you shortly.@k@vS20/09/VTK1_takano1287. ...Hee hee hee hee.``
changeReason: Old invents a 'knife and fork / meal' metaphor not in JPN, which says 'sit in your laboratory chair and await good news, I'll bring the research specimen.'

TextENGNew: U-understood...
There was no question that Miyo Takano-san was my true working partner.
But... I simply could not bring myself to trust her fully.
...It was because a certain dimension of her as a researcher seemed to differ vastly from my own...
...But, I wondered—in what way was I truly different from her?
When all was said and done, I had come here to do exactly what she described: to sit in my chair in the laboratory and conduct research on whatever was brought to me.
changeReason: Old continues the invented 'knife and fork' metaphor; new accurately translates the JPN about sitting in the lab chair and researching what's delivered.

TextENGNew: ...No matter what excuses or evasions one might line up, a fact is a fact.
changeReason: Old says 'don't want to witness a gruesome scene' while JPN says 'no matter what excuses one might line up, a fact is a fact.' Completely different content.

TextENGNew: Nothing more, nothing less.
changeReason: Old is an incomplete fragment ('What she's preparing for me now') bearing no relation to JPN それ以上もそれ以下もない ('Nothing more, nothing less') — content from a different entry entirely.

TextENGNew: And what she was now preparing for me—was it not precisely the thing that stirred my intellectual curiosity more than anything else?
changeReason: Old reduces to flat statement 'is exactly what I want'; new captures the JPN's rhetorical self-questioning about intellectual curiosity (知的好奇心をなによりもかきたてるのではないか).

TextENGNew: What stunning hypocrisy.
changeReason: Old asks 'Who's the hypocrite?' as if identifying a person; new correctly renders 何たる偽善 as an exclamation about the nature of the hypocrisy itself.

TextENGNew: ...Not hers.
Mine.
How was I any different from the past version of myself?
changeReason: Old asks 'how can I say I'm different'; new correctly translates the comparison: 'how was my past self different from her?' (かつての私が彼女とどう違ったというのか).

TextENGNew: I had performed surgery—time after time, without the patient's consent—surgery that could alter the entire course of a person's life. How was I any different from her, standing here now?
...Up until today, I had never once questioned my own past work.
And yet... for some reason, whenever I looked at her, I was filled with unease.
Takano-san was speaking to Okonogi-san on the intercom at my director's desk.
...She was probably summoning him in order to explain the plan she had just drawn up.
I... said nothing, and merely watched without trying to stop her...
I... was turning over the past in my mind, trying to recall the original source of the passion that had brought me to this path...
